# Graphical User Interfaces (GUI's)

There exists a plethora of GUI's that can make using Git a more smooth experience. In many GUI's you can point-and-click your way through everything you need to do, they can help trivialize complicated tasks, they can make beautiful graphical visualizations of complicated commit histories, and so on.

The general advice of this tutorial is to not use a GUI if you don't need to, especially if you are new to Git. Learning Git using a GUI is shortsighted. It might make it easier to get started, but it can have a negative impact on your ability to understand how Git works, which might be problematic in the long run. In continuation of this, some programs that have nothing to do with Git also comes with functionalities to use Git from within the program. It is unclear why it would EVER be desirable to use Git like that. If you want to use a GUI, use a third-party program whose sole purpose is to be a Git GUI.

Below will will briefly list some of pro's and con's of using GUI's and leave it at that.

## Pro's {.unnumbered .unlisted}

**Point-and-click interface**<br>
In GUI's you can point-and-click your way through everything, which makes using Git a more smooth and less technical experience, which is preferable to some people.

**Facilitation of complicated tasks**<br>
Some GUI's can help with complicated tasks. For example, in some third-party GUI's where you login using an account, you can setup your GUI account using your GitHub account, and the GUI will automatically authenticate you to your GitHub repositories. This process is a lot more technical and complicated to do yourself at the terminal.

**Graphical visualizations of commit histories**<br>
Not being able to make a (good) graphical visualization of your commit history might be the biggest limitation of using Git at the terminal. Any decent GUI will provide you with a beautiful graphical visualization of your commit history, which is especially valuable if you are working on a project with an extensive and complicated commit history.

## Con's {.unnumbered .unlisted}

**Point-and-click interface**<br>
Being able to point-and-click your way through everything means that you will most likely be doing exactly that. Which means that you will not learn any Git, and it will make it harder to remember things that you do actually know how to do at the terminal. 

**Facilitation complicated tasks**<br>
Having the GUI do complicated tasks is nice, but again comes with the caveat that you yourself will not in fact learn anything.

**Access to your preferred GUI**<br>
If you are dependent on using a specific GUI to use Git, this might become problematic if you are all of a sudden forced to work in an environment where you do not have access to that GUI (or any other GUI for that matter). The way to make sure this is (probably) not a problem, is to actually learn how to use Git through terminal commands, so that you can always fall back to doing that if necessary.

**Might only have access to a limited set of Git commands**<br>
GUI's do not come with a complete implementation of all Git commands. Some (advanced) Git commands are only available through terminal commands.
